segments.purgatorio-33-g32: Sì com' = 'just as/so as' (Sì = 'so' used adverbially; com' = com + apostrophe, the truncated form of 'come' = 'as'). 'io fui' is passato remoto (1st person singular of essere) meaning 'I was' — together the phrase means 'just as I was'. The order places the comparative phrase before the rest of the clause for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-33-g33: com' io dovëa = 'as I ought (to have been / to be)'; com' = 'come' (as), 'io' = subject pronoun, 'dovëa' is the imperfect/remote form of 'dovere' used here to express obligation or what was proper — 'I ought/was bound to'. The diaeresis on ë is an orthographic/metrical mark in Dante's spelling and does not change the meaning.
segments.purgatorio-33-g34: seco = enclitic prepositional pronoun meaning 'with him' or 'with him/it' (se + co); here it indicates companionship or proximity. It's placed after the phrases it modifies for metrical and emphatic reasons.
segments.purgatorio-33-g35: dissemi is the past (passato remoto) reportative form consisting of 'disse' (he/she said) plus the clitic 'mi' (to me) written together for metrical reasons; it thus means 'he said to me'. The subject is 3rd singular (implied) and the enclitic indicates the indirect object 'to me'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g36: «Frate, is a vocative: 'Brother,' used to address the interlocutor directly. As a vocative it stands outside the verb's argument structure and introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-33-g37: perché non t'attenti a domandarmi omai venendo meco? = a direct question: 'why do you not [t']attenti to ask me now, coming with me?' 'perché' = 'why', 'non' = negation. 't'attenti' is a pronominal verb form with enclitic 't'' (ti), here meaning roughly 'apply yourself / prepare yourself' (the exact verb sense in medieval usage corresponds to taking the trouble or setting oneself to do something). 'a' introduces the infinitive phrase 'domandarmi' ('to ask me'); 'omai' = 'now/at this point'; 'venendo' is a gerund 'coming'; 'meco' = 'with me.' The word order places the pronominal verb before the infinitive phrase and the gerund to mark the timing/circumstance ('as you come with me').
segments.purgatorio-33-g38: This segment is a single punctuation mark (a period) standing alone and contains no lexical material to analyse; it simply separates lines/sentences.
segments.purgatorio-33-g39: 'Come' begins a simile meaning 'as' or 'like'. The construction 'Come a color che ... sono' literally 'As to those who ... are' uses 'a color' (those people) + a relative clause introduced by 'che' ('who') and ends with the finite verb 'sono' (are); the participle 'parlando' (speaking) precedes the verb to indicate the simultaneous action ('who, speaking, are...'). 'Dinanzi a suo maggior' means 'before their superior' with the possessive 'suo' referring back to the group.
segments.purgatorio-33-g40: This relative clause continues with 'che' = 'who/that'. 'Non traggon' is an archaic/spelling-variant 3rd-person-plural present ('traggono') meaning 'they draw/bring'. The phrase 'la voce viva ai denti' is idiomatic: literally 'the lively voice to the teeth' => they do not bring their clear voice up to their teeth (i.e. they hold back their voice, speak quietly or with restraint).
segments.purgatorio-33-g41: 'Avvenne' is the passato remoto (3rd sing.) of 'avvenire' meaning 'it happened'; the dative 'a me' ('to me') follows, so the whole unit means 'it happened to me.' The compact order (verb + dative) is typical in narrative.
segments.purgatorio-33-g42: 'Che' here introduces the subordinate clause explaining what happened: 'that'. 'Sanza' is an archaic form of 'senza' ('without'). 'Intero suono' = 'complete/full sound' (i.e. a complete, audible voice); because the entry for 'suono' in the provided list does not include 'sound', its sense index is given as -1. 'Incominciai' is the passato remoto first person singular 'I began', so the clause reads 'that, without a full sound, I began...'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g43: A vocative direct address: 'Madonna' is used to invoke or address the Virgin/Lady. It stands alone before the reported speech begins.
segments.purgatorio-33-g44: 'Mia bisogna' ('my need' or 'my necessity') is fronted for emphasis; the verb 'conoscete' (2nd-person plural present 'you know') follows with subject pronoun 'voi' (you) placed between the noun and the verb. The construction yields 'you know my need.' Because the provided senses for 'bisogna' are verbal and do not include the noun sense 'need/necessity', its sense index is set to -1.
segments.purgatorio-33-g45: 'E ciò ch' ad essa è buono' = 'and that which is good for her.' 'Ciò' = 'that/what', 'ch'' is the contracted 'che' (relative 'that/which'), 'ad essa' is a dative phrase 'for her', and 'è buono' is the predicate 'is good/suitable.' Word order is neutral: conjunction + demonstrative + relative clause + predicate.
segments.purgatorio-33-g46: 'Ed' is an emphatic form of 'e' ('and'). 'Ella' is the subject 'she' (the Lady) and 'a me' is the dative 'to me', so this is the formulaic 'and she to me:' introducing direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-33-g47: 'Da tema e da vergogna' = 'from/out of fear and from shame' (two prepositional phrases). 'Voglio che' ('I want that') introduces a subordinate clause and therefore normally governs the subjunctive. 'Tu' is the subject of that subordinate clause and the clitic 'ti' is the reflexive object preceding the verb. 'Omai' is an adverb meaning 'now'/'henceforth' (the provided senses do not match this meaning, so its sense index is -1). 'Disviluppe' is a subjunctive form (Dantean/archaic spelling) corresponding to modern 'ti disviluppi' — 'you unfold/come out' (reflexive).
segments.purgatorio-33-g48: 'Sì che' here introduces the intended/resultant outcome 'so that'. 'Non parli' is present subjunctive (negative) or a jussive/optative use meaning 'you should not speak'; 'più' means 'any longer' (stop doing it). 'Com' is the contracted 'come' ('as/like') and 'om' is an archaic form of 'uom'/'uomo' ('man, one'). The final 'che sogna' is a relative clause 'who dreams' modifying 'om'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g49: Sappi is the 2nd-person singular imperative of sapere, used here as a strong command/instruction 'know!' or 'be aware'. Che introduces a content-clause (‘that’). ’l is the elided article il ('the'); vaso is the noun 'vessel' used here in a concrete/figurative sense. The second che is a relative pronoun ('which/that') introducing the relative clause modifying vaso, and ruppe is passato remoto (3rd singular) of rompere, 'broke', placed after its subject (’l serpente) in a normal relative clause.
segments.purgatorio-33-g50: Fu is passato remoto of essere ('was'); e is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; non is the negation 'not'; è is the present tense 'is'. The terse antithesis 'fu e non è' literally 'it was and is not' expresses that something formerly existed but now no longer does.
segments.purgatorio-33-g51: Ma is the adversative conjunction 'but'. Chi here is an indefinite relative/subject meaning 'whoever' or 'he who'. N’ is the clitic form of ne, here 'of it' or 'in that matter' (referring back to the vessel); ha is present indicative 3rd singular of avere ('has'); colpa is 'fault/guilt'. The phrase ma chi n'ha colpa = 'but whoever is guilty of it'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g52: Creda is the 3rd-person present subjunctive of credere used jussively (a hortatory/imperative sense): 'let him believe' or 'may he believe'. Che introduces the subordinate clause. Vendetta di Dio is the subject phrase 'the vengeance of God'. Non teme is present indicative 3rd singular of temere ('does not fear'). Suppe is an archaic, literary form (passato remoto of sapere) appearing at the end of the clause; in this idiomatic, older usage it contributes to the sense of inability to escape or avert the vengeance, so the whole subordinate clause is best rendered 'that he will not escape/evade God's vengeance.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g53: This is a negative future-clause. Non + sarà puts the main verb 'essere' in the future tense (‘will be’). Tutto tempo functions adverbially (‘always, all the time’) and sanza reda is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘without reda’ (reda here functions as an abstract noun, ‘restoration/return’). The relative clause l’aguglia che lasciò le penne al carro modifies l’aguglia (the eagle) with lasciò in the simple past (passato remoto) ‘left,’ and the direct object le penne follows the verb in normal SVO order.
segments.purgatorio-33-g54: Per che here introduces a result or consequence (‘because/therefore’). Divenne is passato remoto of divenire (‘became’). Mostro and poscia preda are predicative nouns linked by e; poscia (‘afterwards’) notes the temporal sequence.
segments.purgatorio-33-g55: Ch’io is a relative/explanatory clause introduced by ch’ (= che). Veggio is a first-person present form meaning ‘I see/perceive’ used here as the speaker’s certainty; certamente is an adverb modifying veggio.
segments.purgatorio-33-g56: E però begins the short main clause ‘and therefore’; il is a neutral pronoun standing for the matter previously mentioned (‘it’), and narro is 1st person present of narrare (‘I tell, I relate’). The clause states the speaker’s act of reporting.
segments.purgatorio-33-g57: A darne is an infinitival phrase where a + dare + ne (pronoun) means ‘to give of it’ or ‘to allow time for it’; tempo is the noun governed by dare; già stelle propinque is a temporal clause ‘when the stars are already near,’ with già (‘already’) and propinque (‘near/approaching’) modifying stelle.
segments.purgatorio-33-g58: Secure is an adjective (feminine form secura used adverbially) meaning ‘safe’ or ‘free from danger.’ The phrase d’ogn’ intoppo e d’ogne sbarro is a paired genitive/partitive structure (‘from every obstacle and from every bar/obstruction’) where intoppo and sbarro are nouns meaning impediment and barrier respectively.
segments.purgatorio-33-g59: Nel quale introduces a relative clause (‘in which’ or ‘during which’). The sequence un cinquecento diece e (implicitly e cinque) enumerates numbers in the old style (literal components: ‘one five-hundred, ten and…’) and functions as a temporal or chronological label (an explicit year expressed in Dante’s archaic enumeration).
segments.purgatorio-33-g60: Messo di Dio is an agentive phrase: messo here (from mettere) is used in the sense ‘sent/appointed’ and di Dio marks divine agency (‘sent by God’). The whole phrase qualifies the person or time mentioned in the previous clause.
segments.purgatorio-33-g61: Anciderà is a future-tense verb (third singular) expressing a decisive action ‘will slay/put an end to’ (the precise lexical shade is archaic). La fuia is the direct object (a feminine noun here); con quel gigante introduces the companion/associate and che con lei delinque is a relative clause where delinque (‘offends/conspires/acts wrongly’) is present-tense and describes the giant’s collusion with la fuia.
segments.purgatorio-33-g62: E forse che introduces a concessive/speculative clause (‘and perhaps that…’). La mia narrazion buia is a nominal phrase: la mia narrazion (my narration) modified by the adjective buia (‘dark/obscure’), with typical noun-adjective order.
segments.purgatorio-33-g63: Qual introduces a comparison (‘such as’ / ‘like’). Temi and Sfinge are presented in apposition as comparison terms; Temi is a proper name here and Sfinge is the mythic Sphinx — both are invoked as authorities of riddling or judgment.
segments.purgatorio-33-g64: Men is an adverb meaning ‘less’; ti is the indirect object pronoun ‘you’; persuade is the present-tense verb ‘persuades/convinces.’ Together they mean ‘it persuades you less’ (i.e., ‘it is less persuasive to you’).
segments.purgatorio-33-g65: Perch’ = perché (‘because’). A lor modo (‘in their manner’) is an adverbial phrase modifying the verb attuia; lo ’ntelletto is the direct object (‘the intellect/understanding’), and attuia (present form of attuare) here means ‘sets in motion / applies / brings into effect’ — so the clause says the narration works on the intellect in their way.
segments.purgatorio-33-g66: Ma tosto (‘but soon’) is an adversative temporal phrase. Fier li fatti le Naiade is an inverted construction in which li fatti (‘the facts’) are the grammatical object and le Naiade (‘the Naiads’) function as the agent that will produce or make those facts manifest; fier here is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning ‘to be made / become / be done.’
segments.purgatorio-33-g67: Che introduces a relative clause describing the Naiads: solveranno is future tense (they will solve/loosen/resolve). Questo enigma forte is the object (‘this mighty riddle’) with forte intensifying enigma, and sanza danno di pecore o di biade means ‘without damage/loss of sheep or of fodder/grain’ (sanza = without, danno = loss).
segments.purgatorio-33-g68: Tu is the vocative/subject pronoun ‘you’ and nota is an imperative (understood: ‘note!’ / ‘take notice!’) addressed to the interlocutor. The short two-word utterance functions as a command to attend.
segments.purgatorio-33-g69: E sì come introduces a simile (‘and just as’). Da me son porte is a passive-looking or stative construction: son (present of essere, ‘are’) + porte (past participle/ adjective ‘opened/brought’ here used as ‘are opened by me’), so the phrase means ‘as doors are opened by me’ (da me marks the agent).
segments.purgatorio-33-g70: Così continues the simile (‘so’). Queste parole segna is an imperative instruction: segna (2nd-person singular imperative of segnare) = ‘mark/record/remember these words’; a’ vivi = ‘to the living’ (indirect object). Del viver ch’è un correre a la morte is a noun clause explaining the content to be marked: viver (nominalized infinitive ‘living/life’) + ch’è (that is) + un correre a la morte (‘a running toward death’), with correre used figuratively.
segments.purgatorio-33-g164: This single word is a heading/title: the noun paradiso used as the proper name of the next canticle. It appears isolated and capitalized, so it functions as a label rather than part of a sentence; there is no article or verb and no inflection to analyze here.
segments.purgatorio-33-g71: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. 'aggi a mente' is an archaic imperative or hortative phrase meaning 'add to (your) mind' / 'bear in mind' (aggi is an old form equivalent to modern aggiungi; a mente = 'to mind' / 'in your memory').
segments.purgatorio-33-g72: Quando tu le scrivi is a temporal clause 'when you write them'; quando = 'when/whenever', tu is the explicit subject 'you', le is the feminine plural clitic referring back to 'these (words)' and scrivi is present tense 'you write'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g73: Di introduces the infinitive phrase 'non celar' ('not to hide'); non is the negation and celar is the infinitive 'to conceal'. Qual hai vista la pianta is a nominal clause 'which (tree) you have seen': qual = 'which/what kind (of)', hai vista is present perfect ('you have seen') with vista as past participle, and la pianta is the feminine noun 'the tree'. The relative ch’ è or due volte dirubata quivi attaches to 'la pianta' (ch’ = che 'which'), with è = 'is' and dirubata = past participle 'stripped/robbed' (here 'stripped' of foliage or bark), or due volte 'twice', quivi = 'there'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g74: Qualunque is an indefinite relative 'whoever/whichever'. Ruba is present indicative 'steals' (3rd person singular) and schianta (here a verb from schiantare) means 'crushes/breaks' (3rd person singular); the two quella/quella point to the trees or parts of the tree ('that one or that one'). The clause names two kinds of wrong (stealing or breaking).
segments.purgatorio-33-g75: Con bestemmia di fatto offende a Dio is the main clause: con = 'with', bestemmia = 'blasphemy', di fatto here means 'by/deed' (di + noun giving the manner/character of the act), offende = 'offends' (3rd person sing.), a Dio = 'against God' (a marking the indirect object).
segments.purgatorio-33-g76: Che introduces a relative clause modifying the act/tree: 'who/which created it holy only for his use.' Solo = 'only', a l'uso suo = 'for his use' (a + definite article + uso + suo), la = direct object 'it/her' (referring to the tree), creò = past tense 'created' and santa = 'holy'. The clause explains God's intent for the tree.
segments.purgatorio-33-g77: Per morder quella = 'for [having] bitten it' or 'because of biting it': per + infinitive here gives the reason or purpose; morder is the infinitive 'to bite' and quella is the direct object 'that/it' (the tree/fruit).
segments.purgatorio-33-g78: This main clause gives the temporal extent and desire: 'in pena e in disio' = 'in punishment and in desire/longing'; 'cinquemilia anni e più' = 'five thousand years and more' (a time span); 'l'anima' = 'the soul' (subject); 'prima bramò' = 'longed previously' (prima = 'before/previously' modifying the verb bramò, past 'yearned'); 'colui che ’l morso in sé punio' is a relative clause 'for the one who punished the bite in himself' (colui = 'he who', che = relative 'who', ’l = elided lo 'the', morso = 'bite', in sé = 'in himself', punio = past tense 'punished').
segments.purgatorio-33-g79: Dorme lo ’ngegno tuo = 'Your wit/intellect sleeps': dorme is 3rd person singular present of dormire, lo 'the' marks the noun 'ngegno' (variant spelling of ingegno = intellect/genius), and tuo is the possessor 'your'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g80: Se non estima ... is a conditional concessive: 'unless you do not judge / unless you consider'. Se non = 'if not' → in English 'unless', estima (2nd person present 'you judge/consider') takes the infinitive clause introduced by per singular cagione esser eccelsa lei tanto e sì travolta ne la cima: per singular cagione = 'for a particular reason', esser eccelsa = infinitive 'to be exalted/lofty', lei = 'she' (referring back to the feminine tree), tanto e sì travolta = 'so much and so overthrown/overwhelmed', ne la cima = 'at/in the summit' (ne = in/within).
segments.purgatorio-33-g1: This is the opening word of a quoted Latin phrase. 'Deus' is a nominative (here vocative/appealing) Latin form meaning 'God' and is quoted intact inside the Italian line.
segments.purgatorio-33-g2: The remainder of the Latin quote; 'venerunt' is the perfect of the Latin verb 'venire' (rendered here as a past action 'have come'/'came'), and 'gentes' is the Latin plural noun 'peoples' or 'nations'. The phrase is quoted literally, keeping Latin word order.
segments.purgatorio-33-g3: 'alternando' is an -ando participle/gerund meaning 'alternating' or 'in alternation' and expresses the manner in which the following 'salmodia' is performed. The two instances of 'or' are the older Italian adverb 'or' meaning 'now' (i.e. 'now three, now four'), and 'dolce salmodia' is a noun phrase 'sweet psalmody' (chanting).
segments.purgatorio-33-g4: 'Le donne' is the feminine plural subject 'the women'. 'Incominciaro' is an archaic past-tense form (Dantesque/old Italian) corresponding to modern 'incominciarono' — third-person plural past, 'they began'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g5: 'E' = 'and'. 'Lagrimando' is a present participle/gerund meaning 'weeping' and specifies the manner in which the women began their psalmody.
segments.purgatorio-33-g6: 'E' = 'and', introducing Beatrice. 'Bëatrice' is the proper name of Dante's guide; here it begins a new clause about her reaction.
segments.purgatorio-33-g7: Two attributive adjectives describing Beatrice: 'sospirosa' (literally 'sighing' or 'sighful') and 'pia' (archaic form meaning 'pious'). Both adjectives agree with Beatrice and characterize her emotional and devout state.
segments.purgatorio-33-g8: 'Quelle' here is a feminine plural demonstrative/pronoun referring back to the women — essentially 'them'/'those women'. 'Ascoltava' is the imperfect 'she was listening' (ongoing past). 'Sì fatta' is a predicate phrase 'so made/so altered' (literally 'so made'), describing Beatrice's state as a result of listening.
segments.purgatorio-33-g9: This is a result clause introduced by 'che' ('so that'). 'Poco più' = 'a little more' or 'almost'; 'a la croce' = 'to the Cross' (i.e. toward the image/attitude of the Crucifixion); 'si cambiò' is a reflexive past (si + cambiò) meaning 'she changed herself' or more idiomatically 'she was changed/turned' — here indicating that Beatrice's look/pose became almost like the Virgin at the Cross. 'Maria' is the proper name 'Mary'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g10: 'Ma poi che' = 'But after' or 'yet when'. 'L’altre vergini' = 'the other virgins' (subject). 'Dier' is an archaic past form here meaning 'gave' (they gave) and governs 'loco' ('place/room'); the phrase 'dier loco a lei di dir' literally 'gave her the place to say' = 'gave her the opportunity to speak.' 'A lei di dir' is an infinitive construction indicating purpose/recipient (for her to say).
segments.purgatorio-33-g11: 'Levata' is a past participle (feminine) meaning 'having risen' or 'raised herself' and agrees with the feminine subject (Beatrice). 'Dritta' = 'upright/straight'; 'in pè' = 'on her feet' (i.e., standing up). Together they describe her physical motion: she stood up straight.
segments.purgatorio-33-g12: 'Rispuose' is an archaic past-tense form (modern 'rispose') meaning 'she answered' or 'replied'. It introduces the words she speaks.
segments.purgatorio-33-g13: 'Colorata' literally 'colored' is used figuratively here as 'flushed' or 'tinged' — a participle describing her face; 'come' = 'like'; 'foco' = 'fire' — the simile 'flushed like fire' characterizes her appearance as she speaks.
segments.purgatorio-33-g14: Latin interjection/word quoted intact: 'Modicum' = 'a little (while)'. Dante inserts Latin liturgical expressions directly into the speech.
segments.purgatorio-33-g15: Latin clause: 'et' = 'and'; 'non' = 'not'; 'videbitis' is Latin future (2nd pl.) of 'vidēre' meaning 'you (pl.) will see'; 'me' = 'me'. So 'et non videbitis me' = 'and you will not see me.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g16: Latin 'et' = 'and'; 'iterum' = 'again' — a brief connective before she addresses the others once more.
segments.purgatorio-33-g17: 'Sorelle mie dilette' = 'my beloved sisters' — a direct address to the other virgins; 'sorelle' = sisters (here sister-nuns or fellow virgins), 'mie' = my, 'dilette' = beloved/cherished.
segments.purgatorio-33-g18: Repeats the Latin 'modicum' = 'a little (while)' used rhetorically to indicate short time.
segments.purgatorio-33-g19: Latin: 'et' = 'and'; 'vos' = 'you' (plural); 'me' = 'me' — 'et vos videbitis me' = 'and you will see me.' The clause balances the earlier 'non videbitis me' with a promise that they will see her again.
segments.purgatorio-33-g20: 'Poi' = 'then/afterwards.' 'Le' is the feminine plural clitic 'them' (the virgins) and comes before the verb; 'si' is a dative/reflexive clitic meaning 'to herself' (i.e. 'placed them before herself'); 'mise' is past tense 'put/placed'; 'innanzi' = 'in front'; 'tutte e sette' = 'all seven.' The clitic order 'le si mise innanzi' literally 'she put them before herself.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g21: 'E' = 'and'; 'dopo' = 'after/behind'; 'sé' = 'herself' (emphatic reflexive). The phrase 'e dopo sé' indicates that, after placing them in front, she herself came after (i.e., stood behind them or followed).
segments.purgatorio-33-g22: 'Solo' = 'only/merely'; 'accennando' is a gerund/participle from 'accennare' meaning 'making a sign/beckoning' or 'indicating' — here it qualifies how she moved the following people (by a mere gesture).
segments.purgatorio-33-g23: 'Mosse' is the past of 'muovere' ('moved' or 'set in motion'); the direct objects follow: 'me' (me), 'e la donna' (and the lady), 'e ’l savio' (and the wise man). The relative 'che ristette' ('who had remained/paused') modifies '’l savio' (the wise man who had stayed behind).
segments.purgatorio-33-g24: 'Così' = 'thus/so.' 'Sen giva' is an older/Dantesque idiom meaning 'se ne andava' — literally 'she went away/continued on' (imperfect 'giva' = was going). 'Se n' (here written 'sen') + imperfect expresses continuous past motion.
segments.purgatorio-33-g25: 'E non credo che' = 'and I do not believe that...' introduces a subordinate clause. 'Fosse' is the imperfect subjunctive of 'essere' (used after 'non credo che' to report a past or counterfactual idea). 'Lo decimo suo passo' = 'her tenth step'; 'in terra posto' = 'placed on the ground' (posto as past participle 'set/placed'). The whole phrase conveys the speaker's sense that scarcely any part of the tenth step had yet touched the ground.
segments.purgatorio-33-g26: 'Quando' = 'when.' The phrase repeats 'con li occhi li occhi' for emphasis: 'with the eyes — (her) eyes —' and is a poetic way to say 'by her gaze.' 'Mi percosse' ('percosse' = struck) is metaphorical: her eyes 'struck' mine, i.e., she fixed her gaze on me. The doubled 'li occhi' is emphatic and stylistic.
segments.purgatorio-33-g27: 'E con tranquillo aspetto' = 'and with a calm/tranquil expression.' The quoted imperative-like 'Vien più tosto' is an older/poetic form of 'vieni' or 'venite' ('come'/'come on') with 'più tosto' meaning 'more quickly/soon' — an injunction to approach or move faster.
segments.purgatorio-33-g28: 'Mi' = indirect object 'to me'; 'disse' = past tense 'she said'. This simply reports the speech act addressed to the narrator.
segments.purgatorio-33-g29: The start of a result/explanatory clause: 'tanto che' = 'so that' or 'to the extent that' and introduces what follows as the consequence or purpose of asking him to come nearer.
segments.purgatorio-33-g30: 'S’io' = 'se io' = 'if I'; 'parlo' is present indicative 'I speak' (Dante often uses the present where modern prose might use subjunctive). 'Teco' = 'with you.' The conditional clause 'if I speak with you' sets up the following purpose/result clause.
segments.purgatorio-33-g31: 'Ad ascoltarmi' = 'to listen to me' (ad + infinitive gives purpose). 'Tu sie' is an archaic subjunctive/optative form (modern 'tu sia') meaning 'you may be' or 'you are' in a jussive/optative sense. 'Ben disposto' = 'well disposed' or 'ready/willing'. Together: 'so that, if I speak with you, you may be well disposed to listen to me.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g81: This is a protasis (if-clause) introduced by 'E se' (and if). 'stati' is a nominal plural (here 'states' or 'fixed conditions') and is the subject of the subjunctive verb 'fossero' (imperfect subjunctive = 'were'). 'li pensier vani intorno a la tua mente' is the expected apodosis: archaic article 'li' marks the plural noun 'pensier vani' (vain thoughts) and 'intorno a la tua mente' is a prepositional phrase 'around your mind' describing their action.
segments.purgatorio-33-g82: 'e' links the clause that follows. The copula 'è' is omitted: 'e ’l piacer loro un Piramo a la gelsa' is elliptical for 'and their pleasure would be a Pyramus to the mulberry' — 'un Piramo' evokes the mythic Pyramus and 'a la gelsa' uses 'a' meaning 'toward/with respect to' the mulberry-tree (gelsa). 'loro' is the possessive 'their'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g83: 'per tante circostanze solamente' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'through/because of so many circumstances, only' and modifies the following clause. 'la giustizia di Dio' is the definite noun phrase 'the justice of God' (here best read as 'divine justice'), the topic that will be recognized.
segments.purgatorio-33-g84: 'ne l’interdetto' places that divine justice 'in/under the interdict' — 'ne' is an older prepositional/locative use meaning 'in' or 'within', and 'l'interdetto' (interdict/ban) is used as a substantive phrase here.
segments.purgatorio-33-g85: 'conosceresti' is the conditional of 'conoscere' ('you would know/recognize'). The phrase 'a l’arbor moralmente' means 'in/at the tree, morally' — 'a' here marks the locus or figurative object ('in the tree'), and 'moralmente' indicates an allegorical or moral reading.
segments.purgatorio-33-g86: 'Ma perch’ io veggio' = 'But because I see'. 'perch’' (perché) here means 'because'. 'veggio' is a first-person present verb of sight ('I see' in older diction). 'te ne lo ’ntelletto fatto di pietra' has the clitic sequence 'te ne' (you IN it/of it) and the noun phrase 'lo ’ntelletto fatto di pietra' = 'the intellect made of stone' (past participle 'fatto' = 'made').
segments.purgatorio-33-g87: 'impetrato' is a past participle from 'impetrare' meaning 'obtained by entreaty' or 'asked for and gotten'; placed after the noun cluster it functions as a parenthetical participle describing the state that follows 'fatto di pietra'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g88: 'tinto' is another past participle ('stained' or 'tinged') placed in the same series of participles; the commas set off a chain of predicates describing the condition of the intellect.
segments.purgatorio-33-g89: 'sì che' introduces a result clause: 'so that'. 't’abbaglia' is 'ti abbaglia' with the clitic attached and means 'dazzles you' (present indicative). 'il lume del mio detto' = 'the light of my saying/words' (genitive 'del').
segments.purgatorio-33-g90: 'voglio anco' = 'I wish/ I also want' — 'voglio' is present tense 'I want' and 'anco' (archaic 'anche') means 'also' or 'too'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g91: 'e se non scritto' = 'and if (it is) not written' — 'se' should be read as the conditional 'if', modifying the implied clause; 'scritto' is an adjective/past participle 'written'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g92: 'almen dipinto' = 'at least painted' — 'almen' (archaic 'almeno') means 'at least' and 'dipinto' is past participle used adjectivally 'painted/depicted'. It offers an alternative mode (if not written, then painted).
segments.purgatorio-33-g93: This final clause is a desiderative/subjunctive purpose: 'che ’l te ne porti dentro a te' = 'that it may carry it within you' where 'che' introduces a subjunctive ('porti' = present subjunctive 3rd sing.), '’l' = 'lo' (it), 'ne' = 'of it'/'from it', and the rest 'per quello che si reca il bordon di palma cinto' explains the means — 'by that which the staff encircled with palm conveys/represents.' 'si reca' is impersonal/passive reflexive.
segments.purgatorio-33-g94: 'E io:' is simply the speaker's turn: 'And I:' introducing Dante's reply.
segments.purgatorio-33-g95: 'Sì come cera da suggello' is a simile 'just as wax for a seal'; 'Sì come' = 'just as'/'as', 'cera' = 'wax', and 'da suggello' = 'for (a) seal' (purpose/usage).
segments.purgatorio-33-g96: 'che la figura impressa non trasmuta' is a relative/result clause: 'where/so that the impressed figure does not change' — 'impressa' is past participle 'impressed/imprinted' modifying 'figura'. Note: 'trasmuta' here means 'changes' (the lexeme given may differ), and the syntax links the simile to the stability of the impressed image.
segments.purgatorio-33-g97: 'segnato è or da voi lo mio cervello' uses an inversion: past participle 'segnato' + 'è' + agent 'da voi' = 'is marked now by you'. 'or' = 'now'; 'lo mio cervello' = 'my brain/intellect' with archaic article + possessive order.
segments.purgatorio-33-g98: 'Ma perché' = 'But why'. 'tanto sovra mia veduta' = 'so far above my sight' (tanto = 'so much', sovra = 'above', mia veduta = 'my view/sight'). 'vostra parola disïata vola' = 'your desired/longed-for word flies' — present tense 'vola' (flies/hurriedly moves).
segments.purgatorio-33-g99: The relative/propositional clause 'che più la perde quanto più s’aiuta' = 'which the more it helps itself the more it loses it.' 'che' = 'which/that', 'più ... quanto più' is a comparative correlative ('the more ... the more'), 's’ aiuta' is reflexive ('helps itself').
segments.purgatorio-33-g132: This segment is a single punctuation mark (a period) marking the end of a line/sentence; it carries no lexical or grammatical content.
segments.purgatorio-33-g133: 'Per cotal priego' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'because of/for such a request' (per = 'for/because of' + cotal = 'such' + priego = 'entreaty/request'). 'detto mi fu' is an inverted passive/periphrastic construction: 'detto' is a past participle ('said'/'told') placed before the auxiliary 'fu' (passato remoto of essere) with the dative clitic 'mi' ('to me'), yielding 'was said/told to me.' The word order (participle before auxiliary and clitic between) is typical of poetic/older Italian.
segments.purgatorio-33-g134: Imperative clause introducing direct speech. 'Priega' is the 2nd-person singular imperative (from pregare) meaning 'ask/beg/request.' 'Matelda' is the person addressed to perform the action. The subordinate clause 'che ’l ti dica' uses 'che' + subjunctive to express the content of the request: literally 'that she tell it to you' (note the enclitic pronoun '’l' = lo/it before the clitic 'ti' — an archaic/pronoun-ordering typical of verse).
segments.purgatorio-33-g135: 'E qui rispuose' = 'And here she replied.' 'E' = 'and'; 'qui' = 'here/at this point'; 'rispuose' is the passato remoto (3rd person singular) of rispondere, used poetically for 'replied.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g136: A simile clause introduced by 'come' meaning 'as' or 'just as.' 'fa' is present indicative of fare ('does'), 'chi' is the generic relative 'one/who,' and the clause 'da colpa si dislega' is reflexive: 'si dislega' (present) means 'frees/release oneself' or 'loosens oneself' from 'colpa' (guilt/fault). The whole line means 'as one does who frees himself from guilt.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g137: A short nominative/vocative phrase introducing the speaker: 'la bella donna' = 'the fair lady' (la + adjective + noun). In context this names Beatrice as the speaker of the lines that follow.
segments.purgatorio-33-g138: A verbless (or periphrastic) clause with past participle used in passive sense. 'Questo e altre cose' = 'this and other things'; 'dette' is the past participle 'said' placed before the copula; 'li son' here functions as 'have been to him' (li is a dative/archaic pronoun 'to him'), and 'per me' marks the agent/author ('by me' in older usage). So the literal order 'dette li son per me' yields 'these and other things have been (told) to him by me.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g139: A coordinate clause 'e son sicura' = 'and I am sure/certain' ('son' = sono). The following 'che' introduces a subordinate clause: 'l’acqua di Letè non gliel nascose' = 'the water of Lethe did not hide it/them from him.' 'gliel' is the fused clitic (gli + lo/lei) meaning 'from him/it to him' in context 'it/them from him'; 'nascose' is passato remoto of nascondere ('hid'), used here in the negative to deny that Lethe erased/hid the matter from him.
segments.purgatorio-33-g140: Simple nominative introduction of the speaker: 'E Bëatrice:' = 'And Beatrice:' with colon marking direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-33-g141: 'Forse' is an adverb meaning 'perhaps.' 'Maggior' is the comparative 'greater' placed before the feminine noun 'cura' ('care/concern'), so the phrase means 'perhaps a greater concern.' The adjective precedes the noun in classical/poetic Italian.
segments.purgatorio-33-g142: A relative clause modifying 'cura.' 'che' = 'which'; 'spesse volte' = 'often'/'frequently' (adverbial phrase); 'la memoria' = 'memory'; 'priva' here functions as a present tense verb (from privare) meaning 'deprives' or 'robs' (i.e., 'which often robs memory'). Word order is typical: relative + adverbial + object + verb.
segments.purgatorio-33-g143: 'fatt’ ha' is the contracted past participle 'fatto' preceding the auxiliary 'ha' (ha fatto la mente sua ...) forming a causative/perfect: 'has made.' The object 'la mente sua' = 'his mind' and the predicate 'oscura' (adjective) is the result: 'has made his mind dark.' 'ne li occhi' is an adverbial locution meaning 'in his eyes' (ne + the article 'li' + 'occhi'), so the whole line reads 'has made his mind dark in his eyes.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g144: Ma (but) is the simple adversative conjunction. Vedi here is a second-person singular imperative (literally 'see! / look!'), used to call attention. Eünoè is the proper name (personified spring/river) and che is a relative pronoun ('which/that') referring back to Eünoè. Là is an adverb of place ('there'), and diriva is an archaic/poetic imperfect or present-like form meaning 'flowed/flows' (here describing the river's motion). The colon marks that what follows are commands or instructions addressed to the river/personification.
segments.purgatorio-33-g145: Menalo is menare (to lead/bring) with the enclitic object pronoun -lo attached (mena + lo → menalo), so it is an imperative 'lead it / bring it'. Ad is the preposition 'to', and esso is the neuter/masculine demonstrative pronoun 'him/it' (the recipient of the river). The whole phrase is an imperative instruction.
segments.purgatorio-33-g146: E is the conjunction 'and'. Come introduces a comparative/way clause 'as' or 'how'. Tu is the subject pronoun 'you'. Se’ is the archaic contracted form of sei (you are). Usa is the present of usare; here it means 'are accustomed' (third person form used impersonally with tu + sei: 'as you are wont/accustomed'). Together the clause means 'and as you are used to (do)'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g147: La is the feminine definite article 'the'. Tramortita is a past participle/adjective meaning 'stunned' or 'overwhelmed'. Sua is the possessive 'its/his/her' agreeing with virtù. Virtù here is used in the sense of 'power/force/virtue' rather than moral virtue. Ravviva is either a present imperative (revive!) or present indicative with an imperative force addressed to the river/personification; grammatically it is the (tu) form of ravvivare, 'revive' or 'restore'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g148: Come is a comparative preposition 'like/as'. Anima is the noun 'soul/spirit', and gentil is an adjective 'noble/refined' modifying anima; together 'like a noble soul'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g149: Che introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. Non is the negation 'not'. Fa is third-person singular present of fare 'does/makes'. Scusa is a noun 'excuse' (here 'makes no excuse' = 'offers no apology').
segments.purgatorio-33-g150: Ma 'but' contrasts with the previous clause. Fa is again 'makes/turns into'. Sua voglia 'its own desire' (sua = possessive, voglia = desire). De (di) introduces origin/possession 'from/of'. La voglia altrui = 'another's desire' (altrui = of others). Tosto che is a temporal conjunction 'as soon as'. È is the verb 'is'. Per segno 'by/through a sign' (per = by means of, segno = sign). Fuor dischiusa is a poetic compound: fuor 'out' + dischiusa 'opened/revealed' meaning 'openly revealed' or 'laid bare.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g151: Così is an adverb 'thus/so' linking the simile to what follows — 'thus' or 'in this way'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g152: Poi che is a causal/temporal phrase 'after/since'. Da essa 'by/from it' (essa refers back to the previous entity). Preso is the past participle of prendere 'taken/seized' and fui is the first-person singular perfect of essere used here after the participle to mean 'I was taken' (perfect passive/compound structure). The whole phrase 'poi che da essa preso fui' = 'after I had been taken by it'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g153: La bella donna: la (definite article) + bella (adjective 'beautiful/fair') + donna (noun 'lady/woman'). Mossesi is an archaic/poetic reflexive past form (mossesi = si mosse) meaning 'she moved herself' or simply 'she moved' (past action). The -si element marks reflexivity/emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-33-g154: E is 'and'. A Stazio = the preposition a ('to') + the proper name Stazio (the poet Statius). Donnescamente is an adverb meaning 'in a womanly or feminine manner' (qualifying how she spoke). Disse is the simple past of dire 'said'. The colon introduces direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-33-g155: Vien is the imperative/present of venire 'come!' (second-person singular command or exhortation). Con is the preposition 'with'. Lui is the tonic pronoun 'him/he' — the direct addressee is invited to go 'with him'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g156: S’io avessi is the archaic contracted conditional subordinate 'Se io avessi' = 'If I had'. S' is a contraction of se, io is the subject pronoun 'I', and avessi is the imperfect/subjunctive form of avere used in the protasis of a conditional ('if I had / were to have').
segments.purgatorio-33-g157: Lettor is the vocative 'reader' addressed directly; it is a shortened form of lettore used in the poetic address.
segments.purgatorio-33-g158: Più lungo spazio da scrivere: più (more/any longer) modifies lungo (long/lengthy) and spazio (space/opportunity), followed by the infinitive phrase da scrivere 'to write' describing what the space would be for — 'more room/space to write.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g159: I’ pur cantere’ in parte: i' (poetic article or contraction here indicates the speaker; historically used for 'io' or a stylistic article), pur 'still/yet', cantere' is the future conditional/old spelling for 'canterei' or 'canterò' meaning 'I would/could sing' (the apostrophe marks elision). In parte 'in part'. Lo dolce ber 'the sweet draught/drinking' (lo + dolce + ber, where ber = poetic infinitive 'to drink' or noun 'drink'). Che mai non m’avria sazio: che = that; mai non = emphatic 'never'; m' (mi) + avria = conditional 'would have to me' i.e. 'would have left me'; sazio = 'sated'. Together: 'the sweet draught that would never have sated me.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g160: Ma perché introduces the reason 'but because'. Piene son tutte le carte ordite: piene (full) + son (are) + tutte le carte (all the pages/papers) ordite (woven/arranged/composed) a questa cantica seconda 'for this second cantica' (ordite a = prepared for). 'Ordite' here is a past participle used figuratively of pages 'woven' for the poem.
segments.purgatorio-33-g161: Non mi lascia più ir lo fren de l’arte: non 'not', mi 'me' (direct or dative object), lascia 'lets/permits', più 'any longer', ir 'to go' (infinitive), lo fren 'the bridle/rein' (figurative restraint), de l’arte 'of the art' (the craft of poetry). The negation + verb + object idiom means 'the rein of my art no longer lets me go on' — i.e., he cannot continue because the allotted pages are full.
segments.purgatorio-33-g162: Io ritornai is the first-person singular passato remoto 'I returned'. Da la santissima onda = 'from the most holy wave' (da + article + superlative adjective + noun). Rifatto is a past participle 'remade/renewed' used predicatively. Sì come introduces the simile 'just as/like'. Piante novelle = 'young/new plants', rinovellate is a past participle 'renewed' agreeing with piante, di novella fronda = 'with new foliage' (di = with/of, novella = new, fronda = foliage/branch). The sequence uses parallel participles to create a vivid image of renewal.
segments.purgatorio-33-g163: Puro e disposto 'pure and disposed' (puro = pure/innocent, disposto = prepared/ready). A salire = 'to climb/ascend' (a + infinitive). A le stelle = 'to the stars' (purpose/destination). The final clause states Dante's spiritual readiness to ascend toward heaven.
segments.purgatorio-33-g115: Veramente 'oramai' are fronted adverbials ('truly' + 'by now') modifying the verb; 'saranno' is the 3rd-person plural future of essere and takes the predicate adjective 'nude'. The subject follows: 'le mie parole' is feminine plural ('the my words') with definite article + possessive; word order (adverbs – verb – predicate – subject) is typical of poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-33-g116: 'Quanto' here means 'as much as' or 'to the extent that'. 'Converrassi' is the literary imperfect subjunctive of convenire used with a prospective/hypothetical sense 'would be fitting' and governs the infinitive 'scovrire' (to expose/reveal). 'Quelle' (those) picks up 'parole'; the prepositional phrase 'a la tua vista rude' literally 'to your rude sight' indicates the audience for the revealing.
segments.purgatorio-33-g117: The sentence begins with coordinating adverbials: 'E più corusco' ('and more sparkling') and the phrase 'con più lenti passi' ('with slower steps') modifies the manner. 'Teneva' is the imperfect of tenere meaning 'kept' or 'held'; the subject 'il sole' follows the verb by inversion, and 'il cerchio di merigge' is the direct object (the sun is described as 'holding' the sun’s midday circle).
segments.purgatorio-33-g118: 'Che' is a relative pronoun linking this clause to the preceding idea ('which'), and 'qua e là' is a simple adverbial 'here and there' giving the spatial distribution of what follows.
segments.purgatorio-33-g119: The simile begins with 'come' ('as'); 'li aspetti' is a nominal phrase where 'li' is the article/pronoun and 'aspetti' (from aspettare) functions like 'those who expect/are on the watch' — i.e. 'as the expectant/watchful ones'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g120: 'Fassi' (si fassi) is a pronominal/passive-like form meaning 'is made' or 'comes into being' (3rd singular subjunctive/poetic form), used here within the relative/simile clause: 'which ... makes itself appear'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g121: 'Quando' is temporal 'when'. 'S’affisser' is an older/poetic pronominal past/fixed form (s' + affisser) meaning 'they fixed themselves' or 'they took up their posts'; the cluster shows archaisms in verb morphology common in Dante.
segments.purgatorio-33-g122: 'Sì come' = 'just as'. 'S’affigge' (s' affigge) is a reflexive/pseudo-passive present meaning 'fixes itself/sets itself' and introduces a comparison to 'chi va dinanzi a gente per iscorta' ('he who goes ahead of a group as an escort'). The conditional/concessive 'se trova novitate o sue vestigge' ('if he finds novelties or their traces') completes the image; note 'se' here is the conjunction 'if' (an instance of older short conjunctions).
segments.purgatorio-33-g123: 'Le sette donne' is the subject 'the seven ladies'; 'al fin d’un’ombra smorta' is a locative/positional phrase 'at the edge/end of a wan/dim shade' ('fin' is a contracted poetic form). 'Smorta' is an adjective agreeing with 'ombra' (feminine singular).
segments.purgatorio-33-g124: 'Qual' opens a simile 'as one who ...'; the sequence 'sotto foglie verdi e rami nigri' gives the position 'under green leaves and dark branches'; 'sovra suoi freddi rivi' = 'above its cold streams'; 'l’alpe porta' ('porta' is the verb 'carries') is the main participle/verb of the simile ('carries the alp'). The syntax matches a common Latin-derived simile structure.
segments.purgatorio-33-g125: 'Dinanzi ad esse' = 'in front of them' (esse refers back to the seven ladies). Proper names 'Ëufratès e Tigri' are the subjects. The verb cluster 'veder mi parve uscir' is a common Dantean construction: infinitive 'veder' + indirect object 'mi' + main verb 'parve' (it seemed) + a further infinitive 'uscir' (to come forth), so literally 'it seemed to me to see them come out of a fountain'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g126: Simply the coordinating conjunction 'e' ('and') linking clauses.
segments.purgatorio-33-g127: 'Quasi' here is a simile marker 'as if'; 'amici' is plural noun 'friends', describing the relationship between the two rivers as they separate.
segments.purgatorio-33-g128: 'Dipartirsi' is the pronominal infinitive 'to part' or 'to separate', used here with the adjective 'pigri' ('sluggish, slow') describing the manner of their parting; the reflexive form emphasizes the rivers' mutual departure.
segments.purgatorio-33-g129: A vocative exclamation: 'O' is the interjection 'oh', and 'luce' ('light') is addressed directly as an epithet for the sun or divine radiance.
segments.purgatorio-33-g130: Continues the vocative: 'o gloria de la gente umana' = 'O glory of the human people'; 'de la gente umana' is a genitive phrase 'of humankind'. Note 'gloria' is used as a noun of address.
segments.purgatorio-33-g131: An interrogative relative/exclamatory clause: the initial 'che' = 'what' ('what water is this'); 'acqua è questa' is the inverted copular clause 'is this water'; the relative 'che qui si dispiega' ('that here unfolds itself') uses the pronominal verb 'si dispiega' (reflexive/middle voice 'unfolds/shows itself'); 'da un principio' = 'from a source/origin'; the last phrase 'e sé da sé lontana' uses emphatic 'sé' twice ('and itself far from itself') to express separation or alienation.
segments.purgatorio-33-g100: This segment is only a single punctuation mark (a period) marking the end of the preceding line/speech; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-33-g101: Perché introduces a causal reply here (’because’). conoschi is 2nd-person singular present indicative of conoscere ('you know'), with the subject tu understood but not expressed.
segments.purgatorio-33-g102: disse is the passato remoto (simple past) 3rd-person singular of dire — a reporting verb: ‘she said’. It simply marks the narrator’s report of speech.
segments.purgatorio-33-g103: Quella scuola is the feminine demonstrative + noun 'that school' (here: the intellectual school/movement). c’ here stands for the relative 'che' (“which/that”) in the older contracted form; hai seguitata is the present-perfect construction (hai + past participle seguitata) meaning 'you have followed' — the participle agrees in gender with the preceding direct object (scuola), which was common in older usage.
segments.purgatorio-33-g104: e veggi sua dottrina: e = 'and'; veggi is a literary present form of vedere (first-person singular 'I see' / 'I perceive'); sua dottrina = 'its doctrine' (sua refers back to that school). come può seguitar la mia parola is a subordinate clause introduced by come ('how' / 'in what manner') with può (3rd-person present of potere) + infinitive seguitar (archaic spelling of seguire) — 'how it can follow my word/teaching.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g105: e veggi vostra via da la divina distar cotanto = 'and I see your path to be so far from the divine.' veggi again 'I see'; vostra via 'your way/path'; da la divina ('from the divine' — an older form for dalla divina) + the infinitive distar 'to be distant'; cotanto quantifies that distance ('so much' / 'to such an extent'). The clause after veggi functions as what the speaker perceives.
segments.purgatorio-33-g106: quanto si discorda da terra il ciel che più alto festina — quanto introduces the comparison 'as much as/as far as'. si is the reflexive/impersonal clitic used with discorda ('differs'); discorda is 3rd-person singular present 'differs/disagrees'. Word order is inverted for verse: the subject il ciel follows the verb. che più alto festina is a relative clause modifying ciel: che = 'which/that'; festina (3rd-person present of festinare) 'hastens', modified by più alto ('higher/most high').
segments.purgatorio-33-g107: Ond' is an archaic form of 'onde' meaning 'whereupon' / 'therefore' here introducing the reply. io rispuosi is passato remoto 'I answered'; lei is the indirect object 'to her'.
segments.purgatorio-33-g108: Non mi ricorda ...: Non = negation. mi = 'to me' (indirect object/clitic). ricorda (present) here has the sense 'occurs to/reminds' (it does/does not occur to me). ch’i’ stranïasse = che io stranïasse — a subordinate clause; stranïasse is an imperfect subjunctive (archaic spelling) expressing the idea of 'have been estranged / made myself strange' in a past/conditional sense. me già mai da voi = 'myself ever from you' (già...mai is an older combination used as 'ever/ever before'). Word order and archaic forms reflect medieval usage.
segments.purgatorio-33-g109: né introduces a second negative member ('nor'). honne is an archaic word here (the provided senses do not match its usage in context), combined with coscïenza to mean something like 'any sense/conscience (of blame)'; che rimorda is a relative clause with rimorda (present) 'that bites / causes remorse' (literally 'gnaws' or 'pricks the conscience').
segments.purgatorio-33-g110: E se tu ricordar non te ne puoi = 'And if you cannot remember it.' E = 'and'. se = 'if' (archaic usage); tu = 'you'. ricordar is the infinitive 'to remember' placed before the finite verb in older word order; non ... puoi = 'you cannot'; te ne = clitic pronouns where ne refers back to the thing to be remembered ('of it/about it') and te (emphatic) 'yourself/you'. The clitics precede the finite verb puoi in this older syntax.
segments.purgatorio-33-g111: sorridendo is a gerund meaning 'smiling' and indicates the manner of speaking. rispuose is passato remoto 'she answered.' Together they mean 'she answered, smiling.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g112: or ti rammenta = 'now remember' (or can be read as an exhortation: rammenta as 2nd-person imperative or present used as a command). ti is the clitic 'to you' (marker of the person addressed). come = 'how' / 'in what manner.' bevesti is an archaic/irregular past form meaning 'you drank' (of Lethe) — the provided lemma senses do not match this verbal sense. di Letè = 'of Lethe' (Lethe is the river of oblivion). ancoi is an archaic adverb (here 'today' / 'this day'); the precise nuance is archaic and not one of the supplied senses.
segments.purgatorio-33-g113: e se dal fummo foco s’argomenta = 'and if from smoke one argues/infers fire.' e = 'and'. se = 'if'. dal = da + il 'from the'. fummo is an archaic form for fumo 'smoke' (the provided senses were for a different form); foco = 'fire'. s'argomenta is impersonal/reflexive (si + argomenta) meaning 'one argues/infers' or 'it is argued/inferred.'
segments.purgatorio-33-g114: cotesta oblivïon chiaro conchiude colpa ne la tua voglia altrove attenta = 'this very oblivion clearly concludes guilt in your will, intent elsewhere.' cotesta = 'this'; oblivïon = 'oblivion/forgetfulness'; chiaro = 'clear/plain' (adverbial/attributive); conchiude = 3rd-person present 'concludes' / 'draws the conclusion'; colpa = 'guilt/blame'; ne = 'in' or 'within' here; la tua voglia = 'your will/desire' (voglia = will/desire); altrove attenta = 'intent elsewhere' (attenta = 'intent/turned toward' — adjectival). The sense: because you drank from Lethe and thus forgot, that clear forgetfulness itself is taken as evidence of guilt in a will that was directed elsewhere.

sentences.purgatorio-33-s5: Just as I was, as I ought to be, beside him, he said to me: "Brother, why do you not set yourself now to ask me, coming with me?"
sentences.purgatorio-33-s7: As happens to those who are overly reverent when speaking before their superior, and so do not let their lively voice reach their teeth, so it happened to me that, without full sound, I began: "Madonna, you know my need and what is good for her."
sentences.purgatorio-33-s8: And she to me: "Out of fear and shame I want you now to unfold yourself, so that you no longer speak like a man who dreams."
sentences.purgatorio-33-s9: Know that the vessel which the serpent broke once was and is no more; but let whoever is guilty of it believe that he will not escape the vengeance of God.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s10: It will not always be a time without restitution for the eagle that left its feathers on the chariot; because of that it became a monster and afterwards prey. I see this for certain, and therefore I relate it — to allow time, when the stars are already near, secure from every obstacle and every barrier — in which (in the year) five hundred, ten and five, sent by God, will strike down la fuia together with that giant who conspires with her.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s11: And perhaps my obscure narration, like Themis and the Sphinx, persuades you less, because in their manner it shapes the intellect; but soon the Naiads will make the facts manifest, who will solve this strong riddle without loss of sheep or of grain.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s12: Note this; and just as doors are opened by me, so mark these words to the living — that living is a running toward death.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s13: And bear in mind, when you write them, not to hide which tree you have seen there, which has now been stripped there twice.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s14: Whoever steals one or breaks another, by that deed offends God with blasphemy, who created it holy only for his use.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s15: For biting it, the soul, in punishment and in longing, longed five thousand years and more first for the one who punished the bite in himself.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s16: Your intellect sleeps, unless you judge — for a particular reason — that she (the tree) is so exalted and so overturned at the summit.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s1: 'Deus, venerunt gentes,'—alternating in a sweet psalmody of three or four (voices)—the women began, weeping; and Beatrice, sighing and pious, listened to them so that she was transformed, almost as if into Mary at the Cross.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s2: But after the other virgins gave her the place to speak, she rose up straight to her feet and answered, flushed like fire: 'A little while, and you will not see me; and again, my beloved sisters, a little while, and you will see me.'
sentences.purgatorio-33-s3: Then she placed all seven of them before her, and after herself, merely making a sign, she moved me and the lady and the wise man who had remained.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s4: Thus she went on; and I do not think that her tenth step had been placed on the ground before, when with her eyes — with her eyes — she struck me; and with a tranquil expression she said to me, 'Come more quickly,' adding, 'so that, if I speak with you, you may be well disposed to listen to me.'
sentences.purgatorio-33-s17: And if "stati" were not the waters of the Elsa, vain thoughts would turn about your mind, and their pleasure would be like a Pyramus to the mulberry; through so many circumstances you would only recognize the justice of God, in the interdiction, in the tree — morally.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s18: But since I see your understanding made of stone, and, once obtained, stained, so that the light of my words dazzles you, I also wish — and if not written, at least painted — that it may carry it inside you by what the palm-girded staff conveys.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s19: And I: 'Just as wax for a seal — so that the impressed figure does not change — now my brain is marked by you.'
sentences.purgatorio-33-s20: But why does your longed-for word fly so far above my sight, that the more it helps itself the more it loses it?
sentences.purgatorio-33-s30: Because of such a request I was told: "Ask Matelda to tell it to you."
sentences.purgatorio-33-s31: And here the fair lady replied, as one does who frees himself from guilt: "These and other things have been said to him by me; and I am sure that the water of Lethe did not hide them from him."
sentences.purgatorio-33-s32: And Beatrice: "Perhaps a greater concern, which oftentimes robs memory, has made his mind dark in his eyes."
sentences.purgatorio-33-s33: But look — Eunoë that flows there: bring it to him, and, as you are wont, revive its benumbed power.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s34: Like a noble soul that makes no excuse but turns another's desire into its own as soon as, by a sign, it is openly revealed; thus, after I had been taken by it, the fair lady moved, and, in a womanly way, said to Statius: 'Come with him.'
sentences.purgatorio-33-s35: If I had, reader, more room to write, I would still sing in part of the sweet draught that would never have sated me; but because all the pages woven for this second cantica are full, the rein of my art no longer lets me go on.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s36: I returned from the most holy wave remade, like young plants renewed with new foliage, pure and ready to climb to the stars.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s25: Truly, by now my words will be bare — as far as it would be fitting to expose them to your rough sight.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s26: And the sun grew more sparkling and, with slower steps, kept the circle of noon, which here and there, as watchmen expect, makes itself apparent, when they set their posts, just as a man who goes before a company as an escort, if he finds anything new or its traces, does — the seven ladies at the edge of a faded shade, as one who under green leaves and dark branches carries the alp above its cold streams.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s27: Before them the Euphrates and the Tigris seemed to me to issue from a fountain, and, as if friends, to part off sluggishly.
sentences.purgatorio-33-s28: “O light, O glory of humankind, what water is this that here unfolds itself from a source and is, of itself, distant from itself?”
sentences.purgatorio-33-s22: “Because you know,” she said, “the school you followed, and I see its doctrine and how it can follow my word; and I see your way to be so far from the divine as the heaven that hastens highest differs from the earth.”
sentences.purgatorio-33-s23: Whereupon I answered her: “It does not occur to me that I ever estranged myself from you, nor any conscience of blame that gnaws at me.”
sentences.purgatorio-33-s24: “And if you cannot recall it,” she replied with a smile, “now remember how you drank of Lethe today; and if from smoke one infers fire, this very oblivion plainly concludes guilt in your will, intent elsewhere.”
